Name: latency_critical_inference
Title: Optimization Landscapes for Latency-Critical Inference in Machine Learning
Short Hypothesis: By leveraging PAC-Bayes frameworks and spectral stability bounds, we can derive enforceable protocols for latency-critical inference that minimize overhead and optimize performance.
Related Work: Recent works such as SNIP and EMQ have explored adaptive mixed-precision training frameworks and training-free proxies for automated mixed-precision quantization. However, these approaches do not directly address the need for latency-critical inference. Our proposal builds upon these works by incorporating latency-aware designs and strict wall-clock budgets.
Abstract: This proposal explores the development of optimization landscapes for latency-critical inference in machine learning. By combining PAC-Bayes frameworks with spectral stability bounds, we aim to create enforceable protocols that minimize overhead and optimize performance. Our approach prioritizes empirical validation, mechanistic transparency, and reproducible designs, ensuring that the proposed methods are both effective and efficient.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Evaluate the performance of our proposed protocol on a range of benchmark datasets, including ImageNet and CIFAR-10.
Experiment 2: Compare the latency and accuracy of our approach with existing state-of-the-art methods, such as SNIP and EMQ.
Experiment 3: Investigate the impact of different hyperparameters and design choices on the performance of our protocol.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed approach may require significant computational resources and expertise in optimization and machine learning.
The effectiveness of our protocol may depend on the specific use case and dataset, requiring further evaluation and refinement.
